ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers starch-based noodles as the closest parent category; no dedicated glass noodle leaf code confirmed.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept confirmed for glass noodles / cellophane noodles; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard add-on serving of cooked glass noodles as offered at a Yong Tau Foo stall is approximately 80 g, representing a small supplementary portion added to a bowl of soup or noodle dish.

nutrient_source.title: Glass noodles, cooked (mung bean starch) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 20113
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked mung bean cellophane noodles (glass noodles) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent cooked weight per 100 g. This matches the food as served — rehydrated/cooked glass noodles. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not carry a dedicated glass noodle entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source with an exact match.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean cellophane noodles
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 26 (low) is reported for mung bean cellophane noodles (glass noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (26 × 16.8) / 100 = 4.4, where carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 80 × 0.21 = 16.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~10 mg per 100 g cooked)
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked glass noodles are very low in sodium and pose minimal hypertension risk; however, individuals should monitor sodium added through accompanying broths, sauces, or seasonings in Yong Tau Foo or Laksa preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 26) due to resistant starch structure of mung bean starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Glass noodles have a low GI of 26 and produce a modest glycemic load (~4.4 per 80 g serving), making them a suitable carbohydrate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and high-GI accompaniments are limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain glass noodles contain negligible fat and saturated fat, posing low cardiovascular risk; the overall dish risk depends on accompanying ingredients such as fried items or high-fat broths in Yong Tau Foo or Laksa contexts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Glass noodles, known in Singapore and Malaysia as Tang Hoon (冬粉), are thin, translucent noodles made from mung bean starch and water. They are sold dried and become soft, chewy, and gelatinous when rehydrated by soaking in hot water or boiling. Their neutral flavour allows them to absorb the taste of surrounding broths and sauces readily. In the Singaporean and Malaysian food context, Tang Hoon is commonly offered as an add-on ingredient in Yong Tau Foo, Laksa, and soup-based noodle dishes, providing additional texture and bulk at low cost. Nutritionally, cooked glass noodles are predominantly carbohydrate (approximately 21 g per 100 g cooked), very low in protein (0.2 g) and fat (0.1 g), and contain minimal sodium (~10 mg per 100 g). Their glycemic index is low at approximately 26, attributed to the resistant starch structure of mung bean starch, which slows glucose absorption. This makes them a relatively diabetes-friendly carbohydrate source compared to white rice or wheat noodles. They are naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal, making them suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. The low fat and sodium content means they are also appropriate for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, provided accompanying sauces and broths are chosen carefully. Tang Hoon is also known as cellophane noodles, bean thread noodles, crystal noodles, and mung bean vermicelli across different culinary traditions in Asia.
common_user_queries: What are glass noodles made of?
How many calories in glass noodles?
What is the difference between glass noodles and rice vermicelli?
Are cellophane noodles the same as Tang Hoon?
How much carbohydrate in glass noodles?
Are glass noodles suitable for Yong Tau Foo?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy exact match for cooked mung bean cellophane noodles (NDB 20113). GI of 26 confirmed in University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Food identity is well-established and unambiguous.
notes: saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — mung bean starch noodles contain no meaningful saturated fat. monounsaturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. polyunsaturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source — glass noodles contain no free sugars. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-derived starch product. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-derived product. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food. The food_name in the input ('Add Tang Hoon (Glass Noodle) 加冬粉') is a menu add-on label; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Glass Noodles (Tang Hoon)'. The cuisine is listed as Malaysian in the input; this food is common across Malaysian and Singaporean cuisine and multilingual naming has been applied accordingly following Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as closest_parent as no dedicated leaf-level code for glass noodles was confirmed in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
